lessons.title: Seeing the Bus: Logic Analyzer vs Oscilloscope
lessons.content:
As a radar tech you already know the rule: pick the instrument that matches the question. The same split governs embedded debugging, and it is the first reflex to rebuild.

A **logic analyzer** samples many channels as 1/0 against a threshold and, crucially, **decodes protocols** in software (UART, SPI, I2C, CAN). It is the right tool when the question is *digital and relational*: "Did the master actually send address 0x76? Did the slave ACK or NACK? Is CPOL/CPHA correct? Which byte is wrong?" With 8+ channels you watch SCL, SDA, CS, and an MCU GPIO marker together and read out the transaction as bytes. You give up vertical detail — it only sees "above or below threshold."

An **oscilloscope** measures a few channels as *continuous voltage over time*. Reach for it when the question is *analog or about signal integrity*: ringing, slow rise times from missing I2C pull-ups, ground bounce, an undershooting reset line, a runt/glitch pulse too narrow to clock in cleanly, or a metastable edge. The scope shows you *why* a level was misread; the logic analyzer shows you *that* it was.

In practice they are complementary. A flaky I2C read: the logic analyzer tells you the NACK is real and on which byte; the scope tells you SDA never reaches a clean logic high because the rise time is too slow for the bus capacitance. Many modern bench tools (and the Saleae) do both: analog capture plus protocol decode.

Mental model: **logic analyzer for "what bytes," scope for "what shape."** When a bus misbehaves, start with the analyzer to localize the failing transaction, then bring the scope to the suspect line only if the bytes look impossible.

items.front: What single capability makes a logic analyzer the default tool for debugging UART/SPI/I2C/CAN?
items.back: Protocol decode: it samples many digital channels and reconstructs the bus traffic as decoded frames/bytes (addresses, ACK/NACK, data), so you read the actual transaction instead of guessing from edges.
items.hint: It is not about voltage accuracy.

items.front: When do you reach for an oscilloscope instead of a logic analyzer?
items.back: When the question is analog or about signal integrity: rise/fall times, ringing, ground bounce, glitches/runt pulses, slow edges from missing pull-ups, metastability — anything where 'above/below threshold' hides the real cause.

items.front: How do the logic analyzer and scope divide a flaky I2C read between them?
items.back: Logic analyzer: confirms which byte NACKs / whether the address is right (the 'what bytes' layer). Scope: shows whether SDA actually reaches a clean high or has slow/ringing edges from bus capacitance or weak pull-ups (the 'what shape' layer).

items.front: Why does a logic analyzer typically offer 8+ channels while a scope offers 2-4?
items.back: A logic analyzer only stores 1 bit per sample per channel (a threshold comparison), so wide channel counts are cheap; a scope digitizes full-resolution voltage at high rate, which is expensive per channel.

items.prompt: Your SPI sensor occasionally returns garbage. The logic analyzer decode looks byte-perfect at every capture, yet the fault recurs. What is the smart next move?
choices: Add more printf calls in the driver
Put a scope on SCK and MISO to look for ringing/overshoot or marginal edges the analyzer's threshold hides
Increase the analyzer sample rate and recapture indefinitely
Replace the sensor immediately
items.explanation: The analyzer only reports above/below threshold, so clean-looking decodes can still ride on a marginal analog edge (ringing, overshoot, slow rise). The scope reveals the signal-integrity problem the digital view masks.

items.prompt: An I2C device intermittently fails to ACK. Which instrument do you grab FIRST and why?
choices: Scope, because I2C is analog at heart
Logic analyzer, to localize which byte/address the NACK lands on before investigating signal shape
A voltmeter on VDD
Neither; just retry in software with delays
items.explanation: Start with the analyzer to localize the failing transaction (is it the address? a data byte? a missing ACK?). Only then, if the bytes look impossible, bring the scope to the suspect line.

items.prompt: On a scope, SDA rises slowly and rounded toward VDD and the device sometimes misreads. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The MCU clock is too fast
RC time constant too large: pull-up resistance too high for the bus capacitance (or missing pull-ups), so the open-drain line can't rise fast enough
The logic analyzer is loading the bus
Wrong SPI mode
items.explanation: I2C is open-drain: lines are pulled high by resistors. Rounded slow rises mean the RC constant is too large (pull-ups too weak or absent for the trace/bus capacitance), so SDA misses the logic-high window.

items.prompt: What does a logic analyzer fundamentally give up compared to an oscilloscope?
choices: The ability to decode protocols
Channel count
Vertical (voltage) detail: it sees only above/below a threshold, not the continuous waveform shape
Triggering on edges
items.explanation: By storing one threshold-compared bit per sample, the analyzer trades away vertical resolution. That is exactly why it can't diagnose ringing, overshoot, or marginal edges the way a scope can.

lessons.title: Stop Lying with printf: SWO/ITM, SEGGER RTT, and defmt
lessons.content:
The biggest debugging-mindset upgrade is to stop trusting `printf`. On bare metal `printf` is a trap: it formats on the target, then **blocks** pushing bytes out a slow UART (a 115200-baud line moves ~11.5 KB/s; a single line can cost milliseconds). That latency sits inside whatever code you instrumented, so it *changes the timing of the very bug you're chasing* and stalls ISRs. It also drags in heavyweight libc formatting.

Three lower-overhead paths, in increasing modernity:

**SWO/ITM trace.** The Cortex-M ITM has 32 stimulus ports; writing a word to a port emits it over the single-wire SWO pin to the debug probe. The CPU does a register store and moves on — no UART, no blocking. Good for `printf`-style strings and event markers, but SWO bandwidth and probe support vary.

**SEGGER RTT (Real-Time Transfer).** RTT puts ring buffers in target RAM; firmware writes a few words into RAM (near-zero overhead, no peripheral), and the debug probe reads that RAM out-of-band over SWD/JTAG while the core runs. Throughput is high and it works even where SWO doesn't. This is the workhorse for fast, non-intrusive logging.

**(MODERN) defmt + RTT (Rust).** `defmt` does **deferred formatting**: the target transmits only a format-string *id* plus raw argument bytes; the *host* does the actual string formatting against the ELF's symbol table. So `defmt::info!("x={}", x)` sends a couple of bytes, not a rendered string — even smaller and faster than RTT alone, and it costs almost no flash for format strings.

Reflex: instrument with RTT/defmt, not blind `printf`. If you must keep `printf`, route it over RTT rather than UART.

items.front: Name the three concrete costs of a blocking UART printf on bare metal.
items.back: 1) Latency: it blocks while shoving bytes out a slow UART (ms per line at 115200). 2) Timing distortion: that delay sits inside instrumented code/ISRs and alters the bug's timing. 3) Code size: heavyweight libc formatting pulled into flash.

items.front: How does ITM/SWO trace logging avoid the printf bottleneck?
items.back: The Cortex-M ITM has 32 stimulus ports; firmware writes a word to a port register and the data is emitted over the single SWO pin to the probe. The CPU just does a store — no UART transmit loop, no blocking.

RTT places ring buffers in target RAM. Firmware just writes a few words into RAM (no peripheral, no blocking); the debug probe reads those buffers out-of-band over SWD/JTAG while the core keeps running.
items.hint: Ring buffers in RAM, probe reads them.

items.front: What is 'deferred formatting' in defmt and why is it so cheap?
items.back: The target sends only a format-string id plus the raw argument bytes; the host reconstructs the full string using symbols from the ELF. The MCU never builds the string, so transmit size and flash usage (no stored format strings) both shrink dramatically.
items.hint: Format on the host, not the target.

items.prompt: A race only appears in the release build. You add a printf in the suspect ISR and the bug disappears. What happened?
choices: The printf fixed the race
The printf's blocking delay changed the timing, masking the race (a Heisenbug); use RTT/GPIO toggle instead
The compiler optimized the race away
The UART hardware corrected it
items.explanation: Blocking printf adds milliseconds of delay that perturb the timing you're measuring. The race is still there; you've just hidden it. Low-overhead RTT or a GPIO toggle observes without disturbing.

items.prompt: You need high-throughput logging on a board whose probe doesn't support SWO well, while the firmware keeps meeting hard deadlines. Best choice?
choices: Crank UART to 921600 and keep using printf
SEGGER RTT: write to RAM ring buffers, probe reads them over SWD with near-zero target overhead
Add more breakpoints
Disable interrupts while printing
items.explanation: RTT needs no SWO pin and no peripheral; the target only writes RAM. It delivers high throughput while leaving timing essentially undisturbed, unlike blocking UART printf.

items.prompt: With defmt over RTT, what actually travels across the wire for defmt::info!("temp={}", t)?
choices: The fully rendered ASCII string 'temp=23'
A format-string id plus the raw bytes of t, formatted into text later on the host
Nothing until the buffer is full
The entire format string every call
items.explanation: defmt defers formatting: only an interned id and raw argument bytes are sent; the host turns them into text using the ELF symbol table. That is why it beats even plain RTT on size and speed.

items.prompt: Your codebase is full of printf calls you can't rewrite right now, but UART blocking is wrecking timing. Pragmatic fix?
choices: Remove all logging
Retarget the printf/_write backend to push bytes into an RTT buffer instead of the UART
Lower the baud rate
Call printf only from the idle loop
items.explanation: You can keep the printf call sites but redirect the low-level output (_write/putchar) to RTT. Same source, but the bytes now go to a RAM ring buffer the probe drains, eliminating the blocking UART cost.

lessons.title: Turning a HardFault into a Source Line
lessons.content:
The payoff skill: convert a mysterious crash into an exact instruction, and time code without disturbing it.

**GPIO-toggle profiling.** The cheapest, most honest timing probe is a free pin. Set it high at the start of an ISR or critical section and clear it at the end; watch the pulse on a scope/logic analyzer. A single store costs nanoseconds, so unlike printf it doesn't move the thing you measure — ideal for measuring ISR latency and jitter (Modules 6/12).

**Decoding faults.** Cortex-M exceptions you'll meet: **HardFault** (the catch-all / escalation), **MemManage** (MPU violation), **BusFault** (bad bus transaction, e.g. bad pointer/peripheral), **UsageFault** (undefined instruction, unaligned access, div-by-zero if enabled). On exception entry the core auto-stacks 8 words: R0-R3, R12, **LR, PC, xPSR** (see diagram). The stacked **PC is the faulting instruction's address** — `addr2line` or your map turns it into a source line. To find which stack holds the frame, check EXC_RETURN bit 2 (MSP vs PSP). Then read the **CFSR** (Configurable Fault Status Register at 0xE000ED28): its MMFSR/BFSR/UFSR sub-fields tell you *why* — e.g. BFSR.PRECISERR with a valid **BFAR** address pinpoints a precise bus fault and the offending address. Many HardFaults are escalated config faults; enabling the individual fault handlers (SHCSR) gives sharper diagnosis.

**Assertions under budget.** `static_assert` (compile-time) is free — use it liberally for sizes/alignment. Runtime `assert` costs flash and cycles, so in tight builds make it lightweight: capture file/line (or just the PC) and trap, rather than pulling in printf.

Reflex to keep: a crash is not a mystery. Read stacked PC -> source line, read CFSR -> cause, read BFAR/MMFAR -> address. Three registers, and the fault becomes a bug report.

items.back: Cortex-M exception stack frame: the eight words (R0-R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) auto-pushed on exception entry, with the stacked PC highlighted as the faulting instruction and a note to select MSP/PSP via EXC_RETURN and cross-check the CFSR.

items.front: After a HardFault, which stacked register points at the source line, and how do you find which stack it's on?
items.back: The stacked PC (offset +0x18 in the 8-word frame). Use addr2line / the map to map it to a source line. Determine MSP vs PSP from EXC_RETURN bit 2 (in LR on entry) to know which stack pointer holds the frame.

items.front: What does the CFSR (0xE000ED28) tell you, and what are its three sub-registers?
items.back: It tells you WHY the fault happened. Sub-fields: MMFSR (MemManage), BFSR (BusFault), UFSR (UsageFault). Bits flag causes like unaligned access, div-by-zero, precise/imprecise bus error, MPU violation.
items.hint: Three byte/halfword sub-fields, one per fault class.

items.front: When does BFAR (or MMFAR) give you a usable address, and what does it contain?
items.back: When the corresponding 'address valid' bit is set (e.g. BFSR.BFARVALID for a precise bus fault). It then holds the faulting memory address — the bad pointer/peripheral address that caused the access fault.
items.hint: Only valid for precise faults; check the valid bit.

items.front: Why is a GPIO toggle a better timing probe than printf for ISR latency?
items.back: Setting/clearing a pin is a single ~ns store with no blocking, so it doesn't perturb the timing you're measuring. You read the high-pulse width directly on a scope/logic analyzer — accurate latency and jitter without a Heisenbug.
items.hint: Cost is one store; printf costs milliseconds.

items.front: Why prefer static_assert over runtime assert in a flash-constrained build?
items.back: static_assert is evaluated at compile time: zero flash, zero cycles, and the build fails instead of the device. Runtime assert costs code/cycles and (naively) drags in printf, so reserve it for things only knowable at runtime and keep it lightweight (trap + capture PC/line).

items.prompt: You hit a HardFault. What is the correct first sequence to localize it?
choices: Add printf everywhere and rerun
Read the stacked PC to get the faulting instruction/source line, then read CFSR for the cause and BFAR/MMFAR for the address
Mass-comment code until it stops crashing
Increase the stack size and hope
items.explanation: The deterministic recipe: stacked PC -> source line, CFSR -> why, BFAR/MMFAR -> which address. Three registers turn the crash into a precise bug report; printf-bisection is slow and can perturb timing.

items.prompt: Your fault handler is the HardFault handler, but CFSR's UsageFault/BusFault bits are set. What does this indicate, and how do you get sharper diagnosis?
choices: CFSR is irrelevant inside HardFault
A configurable fault escalated to HardFault because its specific handler was disabled; enable the individual fault handlers via SHCSR to catch them directly
The CPU is broken
You must clear CFSR before reading PC
items.explanation: If MemManage/Bus/Usage handlers aren't enabled (SHCSR), those faults escalate to HardFault — but CFSR still records the real cause. Enabling the dedicated handlers lets you trap each class directly for finer diagnosis.

items.prompt: CFSR shows UFSR.UNALIGNED set. What kind of code most likely caused it?
choices: A division by zero
A multi-byte access (e.g. a 32-bit load) to a non-word-aligned address, often via a misaligned packed-struct/pointer cast
Writing to flash without unlocking it
An infinite recursion
items.explanation: UFSR.UNALIGNED flags an unaligned word/halfword access that the core (with alignment trap enabled) refuses. Typical sources are packed structs, casting an odd address to a uint32_t*, or deserializing a protocol buffer in place.

items.prompt: BFSR indicates an IMPRECISE bus fault and BFARVALID is 0. Why might the address be unavailable, and what's a practical mitigation?
choices: The fault never happened
Write buffering let the CPU advance before the bus error surfaced, so the exact PC/address is lost; add a DSB/memory barrier after suspect writes to force precise faults while debugging
BFAR is only for MemManage faults
You must reset to read BFAR
items.explanation: Imprecise faults arise from buffered writes completing after the core moved on, so PC/BFAR don't pin the cause. Inserting a DSB (or disabling write buffering) after suspect stores forces the fault to be precise, restoring a usable address.
